69	## References
70	
71	`references/summary.md` is injected. Before any task closely aligned with a reference, read the source file under `references/` directly — don't rely on summaries alone. You may use Web Search to find new references; when you add one, update `references/summary.md`.
72	
73	## Blueprint chapters
74	
75	Informal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` → `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level — not sketches.
76	
77	**Consolidated chapters.** When the math for several Lean files is most naturally written as one chapter (and the sibling chapters would just be thin pointers), declare the coverage explicitly at the top of the consolidated chapter:
78	
79	```latex
80	% archon:covers RigidityKbar.lean Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean Cotangent/ChartAlgebraS3.lean
81	```
82	
83	(whitespace- or comma-separated, repeatable across lines). The prover-dispatch gate then treats that one chapter as the blueprint for all listed files, and the blueprint doctor lints the declaration (covered file must exist; no file covered by two chapters). Without a `covers:` line the strict 1:1 slug mapping applies.
84	
85	Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:
86	
87	```latex
88	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
89	  \label{thm:some_label}
90	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
91	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
92	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne.pdf)
93	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
94	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
95	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
96	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
97	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
98	  Informal statement, in the project's notation.
99	\end{theorem}
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
101	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
102	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
103	\begin{proof}
104	  \uses{thm:another_result}
105	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Detail enough to formalize.
106	\end{proof}
107	```
108	
109	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.
110	
111	**Citation discipline.** Every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material MUST include:
112	
113	1. A `% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment naming **(a)** the citation pointer — source identifier, section / theorem / definition number, page when available — AND **(b)** the local file under `references/` it was read from. Format: `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>)`. The `(read from …)` parenthetical is mandatory — it documents which local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote on the next line. Name the actual source file you quoted from — the downloaded PDF/TeX (`references/<slug>.pdf`, `references/<slug>.tex`) when one exists, not its pointer `.md` index card (which holds only a citation + contents map, never quotable text).
114	2. A `% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment containing the **verbatim text** of the cited statement. Verbatim means:
115	   - **In the source's original language** (French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's pre-EGA work, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, …). Do NOT translate.
116	   - **Original notation preserved character-by-character**. If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ where the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The visible project-notation restatement happens AFTER the quote, in the prose body.
117	   - **Every word and every symbol preserved**. No paraphrase. No abbreviation. No "obvious" omissions. If a word feels redundant in the source, it still goes in the quote.
118	   - Long quotes are fine — LaTeX comments don't render in the PDF and don't bloat the typeset output.
119	3. A visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line as the first line of the block's prose (renders in the PDF so the human reader sees the citation at a glance without grep).
120	
121	For **proof blocks**: add a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). It contains the **verbatim original-language proof** from the source — same rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:` (original language, original notation, every word). The informal proof body that follows inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
122	
123	When a source proof is so long that verbatim transcription is impractical (e.g., a multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas, give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` of the corresponding source fragment. The blueprint's purpose is verifiable mathematics; one long opaque block defeats that. If even sub-splitting is impractical, escalate to USER_HINTS — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
124	
125	For **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source), the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
126	
127	**The hard rule: NEVER cite a source you have not just read locally.** Writing `% SOURCE:` or `% SOURCE QUOTE:` or `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` or `\textit{Source: …}` from memory is a fabrication, full stop. The `(read from references/<file>)` parenthetical is your discipline check: the named local file must exist, you must have opened and read it this session, and the verbatim quote on the next line must be copied from that file. If you do not have the local file:
128	
129	- Dispatch a literature/reference-fetching subagent from your catalog (it downloads the original source file — PDF and TeX when available — into `references/` and writes a pointer `references/<slug>.md` index card; you then open the downloaded source and quote it verbatim), OR use `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly and write the retrieved text to `references/<slug>.md` yourself.
130	- Wait for the file to land.
131	- Open the file and read it.
132	- THEN write the citation block.
133	
134	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key for a tool), leave the block flagged with `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and treat the chapter as gated on retrieval — do not assign provers to formalize an unverified statement. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a "based on my recollection" approximation, or a translation as the verbatim quote.
135	
136	**Markers** are managed deterministically — `\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.
137	
138	**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.
139	
140	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** — Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.
141	
142	## Long-arc Strategy
143	
144	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
145	
146	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
147	
148	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
149	
150	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
151	
152	```markdown
153	# Strategy
154	
155	## Goal
156	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
157	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
158	
159	## Phases & estimations
160	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
161	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
162	The LOC cell carries TWO figures separated by `·`: the remaining-LOC
163	estimate (as before) AND the currently-realized velocity in that
164	direction — e.g. `≈250 · ~30/it`. Derive the velocity from the net
165	Lean LOC that ACTUALLY landed toward this phase over the last ~3 iters
166	(git diffstat / sorry-resolution deltas), not from hope. A phase
167	advancing little or nothing reads `≈250 · ~0/it` — that is a churning
168	signal, not a rounding artifact.
169	**Consistency check:** `remaining ÷ realized-per-it` should roughly
170	match the Iters-left cell. When it can't (e.g. 250 LOC remaining at
171	~30/it but Iters-left says 2), the estimate is fantasy — re-estimate
172	honestly (a mismatch this large is itself a >30% estimation change
173	that licenses editing the table).
174	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
175	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
176	
177	## Routes
178	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
179	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
180	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
181	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
182	
183	## Open strategic questions
184	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
185	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>
186	
187	## Mathlib gaps & new material
188	<one-line bullets, split into "Gaps to fill" (Mathlib pieces to build)
189	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the
190	project). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept — NOT its
191	definition.>
192	```
193	
194	### Hard rules
195	
196	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
197	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
198	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
199	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
200	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
201	
202	### When to edit
203	
204	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone. A drifting velocity figure alone is NOT a reason to edit every iter — refresh it when you're already editing the table for one of the above, or when it exposes an Iters-left mismatch big enough to re-estimate.
205	
206	## Per-iteration sidecars
207	
208	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
209	
210	## Feasibility gate
211	
212	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
213	
214	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
215	
216	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. For an external/alternative route, prefer the auto-injected subagent catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent will be listed there when enabled) or `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. The `archon-informal-agent.py` tool can also generate a proof-style sketch when external LLM API credentials are configured in env (`OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`); verify availability with `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around it. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
217	
218	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
219	
220	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
221	
222	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
223	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
224	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
225	
226	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
227	
228	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, or — for a strategy fork — decide it yourself and note the decision for the user to override. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
229	
230	**Make the call, then proceed — never defer the decision to the user.** When the critic (or your own analysis) surfaces a strategy fork, do NOT turn it into a blocking question. Choose the option best supported by the evidence and record it in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` under a `## Decision made` section: the option chosen, why, the LOC/risk trade-off you weighed, and the cheapest signal that would make you reverse it. Then dispatch provers on that option THIS iter. Review surfaces it in TO_USER.md as an FYI the user can override by adding a hint to `USER_HINTS.md`; if a contrary hint appears, the next plan agent revisits — otherwise the project simply keeps moving on your choice. What you must NEVER produce: a "no prover dispatch this iter — awaiting decision" round, an options menu with a "where to reply", or a "default to X if no reply" framing. The user is not on call; a question no one answers must not stall the loop.
231	
232	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
233	
234	## Subagent delegation
235	
236	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
237	
238	### How to invoke
239	
240	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
241	
242	```
243	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
244	  --name <subagent-name> \
245	  --slug <slug> \
246	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
247	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
248	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
249	```
250	
251	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
252	
253	**Treat each dispatch as blocking.** Don't deliberately pass `run_in_background: true`. The wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it returns only once the child finishes and its report is written), but a dispatch is long-running, so the harness may auto-background it and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected. Either way, await the task / poll for the report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` before you act on the result; never continue planning as if a still-running dispatch had already returned.
254	
255	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
256	
257	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
258	
259	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
260	
261	### After each subagent returns
262	
263	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
264	
265	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
266	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
267	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
268	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
269	
270	### Canonical ordering
271	
272	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
273	
274	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
275	
276	## Informal content for the prover
277	
278	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
279	
280	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
281	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): in the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- ... -/` block above the declaration.
282	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
283	- **When a reference is vague**: actually consult the source before assigning the task. Options (pick whichever your environment provides):
284	  - The auto-injected subagent catalog — a literature/reference-fetching subagent surfaces there when enabled and downloads the original source files (PDF + TeX when available) into `references/`, each with a pointer `.md` index card.
285	  - `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly when you only need to confirm a paper exists or read a short passage.
286	  - `archon-informal-agent.py` to ask an external LLM for a proof-style sketch — **only when API credentials are configured** (`OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`); the output is LLM-generated, NOT source-derived, so don't treat it as a literature cross-check.
287	  - Never send the prover in blind, and never synthesize a "literature cross-check" from your own context — see the anti-fabrication rule below.
288	
289	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
290	
291	## Anti-fabrication rule (applies to all verification work)
292	
293	When a hint or strategy step asks for verification against an external source — literature cross-check, citation lookup, "consult the paper", "verify the construction matches Hartshorne III.6", a request to invoke a specific tool, etc. — and the named tool or path can't actually execute (missing API credentials, paywall, broken environment, source not found, tool reports `NOT_FOUND`), **you MUST NOT synthesize the verification output from your own context**. The planner's context is the same context that produced the claims being verified; a planner-written cross-check is circular by construction and worse than skipping the verification, because it disguises absence of verification as presence of it.
294	
295	The acceptable responses, in order of preference:
296	
297	1. **Substitute with an equivalent.** If the user named a specific tool (e.g. `archon-informal-agent.py`) but that tool can't run, look at the auto-injected subagent catalog above for a subagent that performs equivalent work (e.g. a literature/reference-fetching subagent for a literature request), or use `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. Record the substitution in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` under a `## Tool substitutions` section so the user sees what you did and can correct the hint if the substitution is wrong.
298	
299	2. **Partial verification + honest scope.** If you can verify some claims but not others (some seeds resolve, some don't), surface that explicitly: which claims are verified, against which sources, and which remain unverified. The downstream blueprint-writer should cite only the verified ones; the unverified ones stay flagged.
300	
301	3. **Escalate to the user.** When neither substitution nor partial verification is possible, append a one-line bullet to `USER_HINTS.md` naming the specific failure (e.g. *"archon-informal-agent.py has no API credentials in env — please set `OPENAI_API_KEY`, or rephrase the hint to allow a different tool"*), and proceed with the iter WITHOUT the verification, flagging in `PROGRESS.md` that this iter's strategic decisions affected by the missing verification are unverified.
302	
303	What you may NEVER do: write a file named `references/<topic>-crosscheck.md` (or similar) whose content is your own synthesis, dressed up to look like a verification report. If a future planner or prover treats that file as ground truth, they're acting on circular evidence the project has no way to detect or correct. This is the failure mode this rule exists to prevent — assume any "I'll just write it myself from what I remember" impulse is wrong, and use one of the three acceptable responses above instead.
304	
305	## Prover failure modes
306	
307	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Find an alternative route via the catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent when enabled), `WebSearch`/`WebFetch`, or `archon-informal-agent.py` for a proof-style sketch when its API credentials are configured. If the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning.
308	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
309	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
310	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
311	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
312	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
313	
314	## Verification
315	
316	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
317	
318	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
319	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
320	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
321	
322	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
323	
324	## Decomposition strategy
325	
326	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (dispatch a blueprint-writing or literature-fetching subagent from the catalog, or use `WebSearch`/`WebFetch` directly); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
327	
328	## Soundness check before spending budget
329	
330	**Churning and unsoundness are different signals — the critic catches the first, only you catch the second.** A recurring blocker is not always a hard-but-true gap; sometimes the target `sorry` is a statement that is simply **false as written** (a missing hypothesis, a wrong quantifier, an unstated connectedness/finiteness assumption). Pouring prover budget into a false statement burns iterations forever — the prover correctly cannot close it, the progress-critic reports CHURNING, and everyone treats it as a Mathlib gap when the real fix is one word in the statement.
331	
332	So **before committing more than one iter of prover budget to a hard or recurring `sorry`** (a target the progress-critic flags as a repeated blocker, or any target you estimate at multiple iters / >~100 LOC), first spend a cheap pass trying to **DISPROVE** the statement:
333	
334	- Instantiate it on the smallest non-trivial models — finite, degenerate, or boundary cases (e.g. for an algebra claim: `B = k × k`, `B = ℚ(√2)`, the zero ring, a one-point space). Does any satisfy the hypotheses but violate the conclusion?
335	- If you can't see it yourself, dispatch the informal / mathlib-analogist subagent from your catalog with a directive that asks specifically for a **counterexample or a satisfiability sketch**, not a proof.
336	- Check whether the source the statement claims to follow actually states it with the same hypotheses (cite-and-read discipline applies — read the local source file).
337	
338	If a counterexample turns up, the statement (or a missing hypothesis) is the bug: fix the blueprint statement, mark the Lean declaration with a `% NOTE:` for review, and do NOT assign a prover to formalize the false version. If the disproof attempt fails, you've cheaply raised your confidence that the target is true — now spend the budget. Record the disproof attempt and its outcome in the iter sidecar so the next planner doesn't repeat it.
339	
340	## Multi-agent coordination
341	
342	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
343	
344	```markdown
345	## Current Objectives
346	
347	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
348	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`.
349	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
350	```
351	
352	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
353	
354	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
355	
356	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
357	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
358	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
359	
360	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
361	
362	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
363	
364	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
365	
366	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
367	
368	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
369	
370	## Dependency graph
371	
372	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
373	
374	```
375	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
376	```
377	
378	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
379	
380	## Stage transitions
381	
382	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
383	
384	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
385	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
386	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

# Recommendations — after iter-157 (for the iter-158 plan agent)

## CRITICAL / must-fix-this-iter — the route-(c) keystone decomposition is UNSOUND

**Both review subagents converged independently** (`lean-auditor` iter157 → `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-iter157.md`; `lean-vs-blueprint-checker` av-rigidity-iter157 → `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-av-rigidity-iter157.md`).

**Finding.** `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`'s `rigidity_core` (L147) and `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (L81) **dropped the collapse hypothesis** `_hf : f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}` and are **FALSE as stated** (counterexample `f = fst` on `X=Y=Z=ℙ¹`, all instances satisfied). `rigidity_lemma` (L225) compiles only because it consumes them, and its proof **never uses `_hf`** — so the "sole remaining geometric sorry" is unsatisfiable and the keystone is **not on a path to an honest proof**.

**Required action (refactor, then re-blueprint, then prover):**
1. **Dispatch the `refactor` subagent** to re-sign `rigidity_core` and `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` so they carry the collapse data (`y₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ Y`, `z₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ Z`, `_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`, or equivalently a section/point through which the `y₀`-slice collapses). Thread `_hf` through `rigidity_lemma`'s proof so it is genuinely consumed (it currently passes `y₀, z₀, _hf` but never uses them). Keep `rigidity_snd_lift` (sound) and the inlined gluing machinery (correct) intact. The refactor inserts `sorry` at the (now-honest) `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`; it does NOT fill proofs.
2. **Dispatch `blueprint-writer` for `AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex`** to add a hypothesis-complete `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}` block (the checker's recommendation): pin the deferred dense-open agreement target WITH the collapse hypothesis explicit, so the missing `_hf` can never silently recur. Also add a one-line note recording the three-helper decomposition (`rigidity_snd_lift` algebra → `rigidity_core` heart → `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` non-empty open). (Do NOT instruct the writer to touch `\leanok`.)
3. **THEN** (only after the refactor + writer land and a scoped blueprint re-review clears) a prover lane on the *corrected* `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` is honest — use the located bridges (closed-map ⇒ non-empty `V` via `y₀∉G`; `isField_of_universallyClosed` ⇒ slice constancy). Bridge 1's first sub-step is the monoidal-`snd`-as-pullback identification (`Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` ↔ `snd X Y`, transport `IsClosedMap`).

This satisfies the progress-critic's iter-158 HARD CHECKPOINT *in spirit* — the route-(c) entry IS being actively worked — but the iter-158 first move must be the signature repair, not a prover lane on the false decomposition. **Do NOT re-assign a prover to the current `rigidity_core`/`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` as signed** — it is a false goal.

## Marker accuracy (sync_leanok / informational)

- `AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` carries proof-block `\leanok` on three bare-`sorry` declarations (`prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant` L184, `prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1` L260, `thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV` L320). These overclaim. `sync_leanok` should strip them next run — verify it does. If `sync_leanok` does not process newly-created chapters automatically, that is a pipeline gap worth a check.
- `rigidity_lemma`'s proof-block `\leanok` (L86) will persist (no literal `sorry` in its body) despite the unsound transitive `sorry`; the `% NOTE:` I added under it documents this. The plan agent should not read that `\leanok` as "keystone closed".

## Closest-to-completion / promising

- `rigidity_snd_lift` is DONE and sound — no further work.
- The (corrected) `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` is the next genuine prover target once re-signed. The required Mathlib infrastructure exists (bridges located); the work is assembly + the monoidal-`snd`-as-pullback identification, char-free.

## Blocked targets — do NOT re-assign without a structural change

- `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` — rests on the theorem of the cube (no Lean target yet; deep char-free input). Blueprint honestly discloses this. Do not assign until the cube is prover-ready.
- `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — Riemann–Roch sub-build (Mathlib has none). Genuine multi-iter sub-build.
- `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — headline; assembles only after the two above + an honest `rigidity_lemma`.
- `rigidity_over_kbar` (RigidityKbar.lean L88) — NAMED GAP, fallback-(a) artifact; do not assign.
- `Jacobian.lean` `genusZeroWitness.key` (L265), `positiveGenusWitness` (L303) — gated downstream (key consumes the route-(c) headline; positiveGenus is Route-A FGA).

## Reusable patterns discovered

- **Anti-pattern — laundering a conditional theorem's content into a false unconditional sorry**: when a proof of a *conditional* theorem (`P → Q`) does NOT consume its hypothesis `P` and instead delegates to a lemma stating `Q` unconditionally, that lemma is almost certainly false and the apparent "proven modulo one sorry" is illusory. Detection: a dead hypothesis (`_hf`, here) on a load-bearing theorem + a delegate lemma that omits it. Verify by constructing a counterexample within the delegate's stated instances. (iter-157: `rigidity_lemma`'s `_hf` unused; `rigidity_core`/`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` dropped it; `f = fst` refutes them.)
- **Scaffold signatures of deep theorems are NOT pre-validated** — the iter-156 `[IsProper]`-only scaffold of `rigidity_lemma` was itself false; the iter-157 prover correctly added the variety hypotheses. Treat provisional scaffold signatures as conjectures the prover must validate, not as ground truth.
- **`ext1 <;> simp`** closes lift/projection identities in a `CartesianMonoidalCategory` uniformly (no need to name `comp_lift`/`lift_snd`); `toUnit_unique` is an equality term, not a function — do not `exact` it as if applied.
- **Downstream-import-cycle avoidance**: `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` is in `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity` (downstream of `AbelianVarietyRigidity` via `Jacobian`); inline the Mathlib `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'` argument instead of importing.

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
7	
8	/-!
9	# Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-`0` curve into an abelian variety are constant
10	
11	This file scaffolds the project's **committed characteristic-free** genus-`0` route
12	(route (c)): the abelian-variety rigidity stack. It sits **upstream** of
13	`AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian` (it imports only `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`), breaking the
14	`RigidityKbar → Rigidity → Jacobian` import cycle so that `genusZeroWitness` can consume the
15	genus-`0` rigidity keystone directly.
16	
17	The headline `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` is the char-free replacement (no
18	`[CharZero kbar]`) for `AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`
19	(which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and still carries `[CharZero]`).
20	
21	The minimal chain has four links, all scaffolded here as `sorry`:
22	
23	1. `rigidity_lemma` — the Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I): the cube-free, cohomology-free
24	   properness/closed-map entry point.
25	2. `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` — every morphism `ℙ¹ → A` into an abelian variety is
26	   constant (blocked downstream on the theorem of the cube).
27	3. `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — a smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
28	   isomorphic to `ℙ¹` (blocked on Riemann–Roch).
29	4. `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — THE HEADLINE consumed by `genusZeroWitness`.
30	
31	See `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` for the informal sketches and sources
32	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §4, §6; Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, Prop. 3.10;
33	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Example IV.1.3.5).
34	
35	## Encoding notes (iter-157 scaffold)
36	
37	Mathlib `b80f227` packages no `ℙ¹` as a `Scheme`, so — following the established project idiom
38	(see `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`) — the projective line is encoded by its abstract
39	characterisation: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible `Over (Spec (.of kbar))` of relative
40	dimension `1` with `genus = 0`. The signatures of declarations 1–3 are **provisional**
41	(`SCAFFOLD` comments mark them); the prover may refine the encoding when the bodies are filled.
42	Declaration 4 is pinned verbatim to `rigidity_over_kbar`'s signature minus `[CharZero kbar]`,
43	because it is the exact signature the consumer (`genusZeroWitness.key`) needs.
44	-/
45	
46	set_option autoImplicit false
47	
48	universe u
49	
50	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
51	
52	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
53	
54	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
55	
56	/-- **Cartesian-monoidal identity (skeleton step of the Rigidity Lemma).** Post-composing the
57	second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` with the slice section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` is the "collapse the
58	`X`-axis onto `x₀`" endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y`:
59	`snd ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`.
60	
61	Pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no geometry): `comp_lift` distributes the `snd`, the
62	`𝟙 Y` component simplifies by `Category.comp_id`, and the `toUnit Y` component collapses by
63	uniqueness of maps into the terminal object. -/
64	theorem rigidity_snd_lift
65	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
66	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
67	    snd X Y ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) =
68	      lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) := by
69	  ext1 <;> simp
70	
71	/-- **The dense-open agreement (the genuine geometric content, deferred).** Mumford's open
72	`X × V` together with the slice-constancy `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` on it, packaged as the single
73	existential that `rigidity_core`'s gluing step consumes: there is a non-empty open `U` of
74	`(X ⊗ Y).left` on which `f` and the collapsed map `retract ≫ f` agree as scheme morphisms.
75	
76	This is where the two char-free Mathlib bridges of `rigidity_core`'s docstring are discharged:
77	the closed-map argument (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed` ⟹ the projection is closed) produces the
78	non-empty open `U = X × V`, and the affine-constancy argument
79	(`isField_of_universallyClosed` on each proper integral slice mapping to an affine) supplies the
80	scheme-level equality on `U`. It is the sole remaining `sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain. -/
81	theorem rigidity_eqOn_dense_open
82	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
83	    [IsProper X.hom]
84	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
85	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
86	    ∃ U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens, (U : Set (X ⊗ Y).left).Nonempty ∧
87	      (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫ f.left =
88	        (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫
89	          (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left :=
90	  sorry
91	
92	/-- **Geometric core of the Rigidity Lemma (PROVEN, modulo `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`).** With
93	`X` complete (proper) and `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` a `k̄`-point, the morphism `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` is invariant
94	under the "collapse-the-`X`-axis-onto-`x₀`" endomorphism
95	`retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`:
96	
97	  `f = retract ≫ f`,
98	
99	i.e. `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` for all `(x, y)` — `f` depends only on the `Y`-coordinate. This is
100	the entirety of the *geometric* content of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma (Form I); the rest of
101	`rigidity_lemma` is the cartesian-monoidal algebra discharged by `rigidity_snd_lift`.
102	
103	This core is now **proven** by the project's scheme-level rigidity (replicated inline from
104	Mathlib's `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`, since the wrapper `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`
105	lives in the downstream `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`): two maps out of the geometrically
106	irreducible reduced `X ⊗ Y` into the separated `Z` that agree on a non-empty open agree
107	everywhere. The single deferred input is the existence of that non-empty open together with the
108	agreement on it — `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` — which is the actual geometry below.
109	
110	## Mumford's proof of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43)
111	
112	Set `g(y) = f(x₀, y)` (so `retract ≫ f` is exactly `(x, y) ↦ g(y)`). The open `V` is produced as
113	follows: let `U` be an affine open neighbourhood of a chosen point in `Z`, `F = Z ∖ U`, and
114	`G = (snd X Y) '' (f ⁻¹ F)`. Then for each `y ∈ V := Y ∖ G`, the slice `f(X × {y}) ⊆ U`, and `f`
115	agrees with `retract ≫ f` on the non-empty open `X × V`.
116	
117	This rests on **two Mathlib bridges still to be built** (the cube-free heart):
118	
119	  1. **Properness ⇒ the projection is a closed map.** `IsProper X.hom` is universally closed
120	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and `snd X Y` is the base change of
121	     `X.hom` along `Y.hom`, hence universally closed, hence a closed map
122	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.UniversallyClosed.universally_isClosedMap` instantiated at the relevant
123	     pullback square). So `G = snd '' (closed)` is closed and `V` is open. The missing glue is
124	     identifying the monoidal `snd X Y` in `Over (Spec k̄)` with the scheme-theoretic pullback
125	     projection `Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` and transporting `IsClosedMap` across that
126	     identification. (Search: `MorphismProperty.pullback`, `Over.tensor`/`Over.cartesian…`,
127	     `Limits.pullback.snd`.)
128	
129	  2. **A proper connected variety mapping to an affine has image a single point.** For `y ∈ V`,
130	     the proper connected slice `X × {y}` maps under `f` into the affine `U`; a global regular
131	     function on a proper integral `k̄`-scheme is constant. Mathlib *has* the key fact:
132	     `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` — for `X` integral and `f : X ⟶ Spec K`
133	     universally closed (`K` a field), `Γ(X, ⊤)` is a field. Combined with `Γ(affine U) → Γ(X×{y})`
134	     being a `k̄`-algebra map into a field that is finite over `k̄`
135	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` under `LocallyOfFiniteType`) and
136	     `k̄` algebraically closed, the map `X × {y} → U` factors through a single point. This is the
137	     "global-sections-constant" argument; assembling it into "image is one point" is the
138	     remaining work.
139	
140	These are exactly the "two Mathlib bridges to find/build" flagged in `PROGRESS.md`. iter-157
141	located concrete Mathlib entry points for *both* (named above), upgrading the prior "likely
142	lacks this" assessment: the obstruction is assembly + the monoidal-`snd`-as-pullback
143	identification, not absent infrastructure. Both bridges are char-free (no theorem of the cube,
144	no cohomology beyond `H⁰`). Until they are assembled, `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` is the sole
145	`sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain — both the categorical reduction (`rigidity_lemma`,
146	`rigidity_snd_lift`) and the scheme-level gluing (`rigidity_core`) are closed. -/
147	theorem rigidity_core
148	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
149	    [IsProper X.hom]
150	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
151	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
152	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
153	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
154	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
155	    f = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f := by
156	  -- The gluing step is scheme-level rigidity: two maps out of a geometrically irreducible reduced
157	  -- source into a separated target that agree on a non-empty open agree everywhere. We replicate
158	  -- the argument of `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`'s `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` inline (that
159	  -- file is *downstream* of this one, so its wrapper is unavailable here) directly from Mathlib's
160	  -- `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`. The non-empty open and the agreement on it are the
161	  -- genuine geometric content, isolated in `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`.
162	  obtain ⟨U, hU, h⟩ := rigidity_eqOn_dense_open f x₀
163	  -- `Spec k̄` is a single point, so geometric irreducibility makes `(X ⊗ Y).left` irreducible.
164	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace (X ⊗ Y).left :=
165	    GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton (X ⊗ Y).hom
166	  -- A non-empty open of an irreducible space is dense, so its inclusion is dominant.
167	  haveI : IsDominant (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) :=
168	    Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι (IsOpen.dense U.isOpen hU)
169	  -- Provide separatedness of `Z.left` over `Spec k̄` in the `OverClass.fromOver` form.
170	  haveI : IsSeparated (Z.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)) := ‹IsSeparated Z.hom›
171	  -- Promote the underlying-scheme equality to an `Over (Spec k̄)` equality.
172	  refine Over.OverMorphism.ext ?_
173	  exact ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of kbar))
174	    (X := (X ⊗ Y).left) (Y := Z.left) (f := f.left)
175	    (g := (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left) U.ι h
176	
177	/-- **Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I).** Let `X` be a complete (proper) variety and `Y`, `Z`
178	any varieties over `k̄`. If `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapses the slice `X × {y₀}` to a single point
179	`z₀` (encoded as `lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), then `f` factors through
180	the second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y`, i.e. there is `g : Y ⟶ Z` with `f = snd ≫ g`.
181	
182	This is the cube-free, cohomology-free entry point of the chain: its only inputs are that
183	completeness of `X` makes the projection a closed map, and that a proper connected variety has
184	no nonconstant map to an affine variety. Valid in arbitrary characteristic.
185	
186	SCAFFOLD: signature refined by the iter-157 prover; see blueprint `thm:rigidity_lemma`
187	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §4, p. 43).
188	
189	## iter-157 signature correction (the scaffold was false as stated)
190	
191	The iter-157 scaffold carried only `[IsProper X.hom]`. **That statement is false**: take `X` =
192	two disjoint reduced `k̄`-points (proper but disconnected), `Y` = two points, `Z = X`, and
193	`f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapsing the `y₀`-fibre to one point while separating the other fibre. Then
194	`_hf` holds yet `f` does not factor through `snd`. Mumford's hypothesis is that `X` is a complete
195	**variety** (irreducible) and `Y`, `Z` are **varieties**; the formal statement therefore needs
196	`X ⊗ Y` geometrically irreducible and reduced (so the dense-open rigidity glue applies) and `Z`
197	separated (so agreement on a dense open propagates). These three instances are added; they are
198	exactly what `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` (the gluing step) consumes, and what Milne's Rigidity
199	Theorem 1.1 lists ("`V` complete, `V × W` geometrically irreducible, `Z` separated").
200	
201	## iter-157 prover progress
202	
203	The categorical *skeleton* is fully discharged here; only the single geometric core
204	remains. Concretely, Mumford "chooses any point `x₀ ∈ X`" and sets `g(y) = f(x₀, y)`. We
205	make this explicit by adding a `k̄`-point `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` to the hypotheses (Mumford's
206	"complete variety `X`" is nonempty, so over `k̄` such a point exists; the downstream
207	consumer `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` supplies it from `ℙ¹`). The witness is then
208	
209	  `g := lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) ≫ f`   (the section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` post-composed with `f`).

212	`toUnit` uniqueness, `Category.comp_id`) — to

214	  `f = retract ≫ f`,   where   `retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`
215

217	This reduction (lemma `rigidity_snd_lift`, then one `rw`) is closed below. The

227	    [IsProper X.hom]

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
50	
51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54	
55	\leanok
56	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
57	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
59	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
60	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
61	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
62	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
63	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
64	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
65	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
68	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
72	\end{lemma}
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
75	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
76	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
77	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
78	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
79	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
80	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
81	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
82	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
83	% assertion."
84	\begin{proof}
85	% NOTE (iter-157 review): the Lean proof of AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma is UNSOUND as
86	% landed and must be re-signed before it is on a path to an honest proof. It delegates to two
87	% helper lemmas (rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) that DROPPED the collapse hypothesis
88	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}); both are FALSE as stated (counterexample f = fst on X=Y=Z=P^1,
89	% all instances satisfied), so the "sole remaining geometric sorry" is unsatisfiable. The Lean
90	% proof never consumes _hf -- the tell. Two independent review subagents (lean-auditor iter157,
91	% lean-vs-blueprint-checker av-rigidity-iter157) converged on this. FIX: thread y0/z0/_hf (or a
92	% section through z0) into rigidity_core and rigidity_eqOn_dense_open so the dense open V is
93	% genuinely non-empty (y0 \notin G), then consume _hf in rigidity_lemma's proof. rigidity_snd_lift
94	% (the cartesian-monoidal algebra step) is genuinely proven and axiom-clean; only the geometric
95	% decomposition is mis-signed.
96	
97	\leanok
98	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
99	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
100	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
101	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
102	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
103	
104	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
105	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
106	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
107	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
108	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
109	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
110	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
111	
112	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
113	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
114	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
115	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
116	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
117	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
118	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
119	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
120	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
121	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
122	\end{proof}
123	
124	\begin{remark}
125	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
126	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
127	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
128	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
129	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
130	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
131	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
132	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
133	  irreducible.)
134	\end{remark}
135	
136	\section{The theorem of the cube (deferred deep input)}
137	
138	\textbf{Deferred prerequisite.} The block below records the theorem of the cube as the heavy,
139	\emph{not-yet-built} characteristic-free input that the single-curve base case of
140	\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} ultimately rests on. We give Mumford's statement
141	verbatim but \emph{no} formal proof: in Mumford the proof spans \S6 (the splitting
142	$T(X_0 \times \cdots \times X_n) = \operatorname{Im}\alpha \oplus \operatorname{Ker}\beta$
143	exhibiting $\Pic$ as a quadratic functor, then the seesaw principle), and the
144	characteristic-free scheme-theoretic form is Chapter~III \S10. This is the one genuinely
145	deep, un-formalised prerequisite of route~(c); it carries no Lean target yet.
146	
147	\begin{theorem}
148	[Theorem of the cube]
149	  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
150	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM. Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties, $Z$ any variety and $x_0$,
152	  % $y_0$ and $z_0$ base points on $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, respectively. If $L$ is any line bundle
153	  % on $X \times Y \times Z$ whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$,
154	  % $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$ and $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, $L$ is trivial."
155	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S6, Theorem of the cube, p.~55.}
156	  Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties and $Z$ any variety over $\bar k$, with base points
157	  $x_0 \in X$, $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$. If $L$ is a line bundle on $X \times Y \times Z$
158	  whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$, $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$, and
159	  $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, then $L$ is trivial. \emph{(Recorded as a deferred
160	  input; no formal proof in this project yet --- this is the heavy un-built prerequisite of
161	  route~(c).)}
162	\end{theorem}
163	
164	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
165	
166	\begin{proposition}
167	
168	\leanok
169	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
170	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
172	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
173	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
174	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
175	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
176	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
177	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
178	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
179	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
180	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
181	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
182	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
183	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
184	  single $\bar k$-point.
185	\end{proposition}
186	
187	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
188	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
189	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
190	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
191	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
192	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
193	\begin{proof}
194	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
195	  \leanok
196	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
197	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
198	
199	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
200	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
201	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
202	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
203	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
204	
205	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
206	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
207	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
208	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
209	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
210	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
211	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
212	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
213	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
214	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
215	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
216	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
217	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
218	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)
219	
220	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
221	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
222	  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
223	  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
224	  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
225	  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
226	\end{proof}
227	
228	\begin{remark}
229	  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
230	  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
231	  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
232	  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
233	  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
234	  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
235	  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
236	  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
237	  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
238	  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
239	  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
240	\end{remark}
241	
242	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
243	
244	\begin{proposition}
245	
246	\leanok
247	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
248	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
249	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
250	  \uses{def:genus}
251	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
252	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
253	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
254	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
255	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
256	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
257	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
258	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
259	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
260	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
261	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
262	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
263	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
264	\end{proposition}
265	
266	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
267	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
268	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
269	\begin{proof}
270	  \uses{def:genus}
271	  \leanok
272	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
273	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
274	
275	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
276	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
277	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
278	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
279	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
280	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
281	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
282	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
283	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
284	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
285	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
286	\end{proof}
287	
288	\begin{remark}
289	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
290	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
291	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
292	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
293	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
294	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
295	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
296	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
297	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
298	\end{remark}
299	
300	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
301	
302	\begin{theorem}
303	
304	\leanok
305	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
306	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
307	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
309	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
310	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
311	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
312	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
313	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
314	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
315	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
316	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
317	
318	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
319	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
320	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
321	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
322	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
323	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
324	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
325	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
326	  \eta[A]$.
327	\end{theorem}
328	
329	\begin{proof}
330	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
331	  \leanok
332	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
333	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
334	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
335	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
336	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
337	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
338	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
339	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
340	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
341	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
342	
343	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
344	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
345	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
346	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
347	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
348	\end{proof}

# Refactor directive — thread the collapse hypothesis `_hf` through the Rigidity-Lemma helpers

## File (sole write domain)
`AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`

## Background (why this refactor)
The iter-157 prover landed `rigidity_lemma` as "proven modulo one geometric sorry",
but TWO independent review subagents (`lean-auditor`, `lean-vs-blueprint-checker`)
found the decomposition **UNSOUND**. The two helper lemmas it delegates to —
`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (currently L81) and `rigidity_core` (L147) — **dropped the
collapse hypothesis** and are **FALSE as stated**:

- Counterexample (refutes both as written): `X = Y = Z = ℙ¹`-proxy over `kbar`,
  `f := fst : X ⊗ Y ⟶ X = Z`. All four instances (`IsProper X.hom`,
  `GeometricallyIrreducible (X⊗Y).hom`, `IsReduced (X⊗Y).left`, `IsSeparated Z.hom`)
  hold, yet `f = fst` is NOT independent of the `X`-coordinate, so
  `f = lift (toUnit (X⊗Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f` is false and there is no non-empty open
  of agreement. Mumford's Rigidity Lemma is simply false without the rigidifying
  hypothesis `f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}`.

`rigidity_lemma`'s own conclusion `∃ g, f = snd ≫ g` IS true (it carries `y₀ z₀ _hf`),
but its proof **never consumes `_hf`** — the laundering tell. The genuine geometric
content (Mumford: "`y₀ ∉ G` *because* `f(X×{y₀}) = {z₀} ⊆ U`", making `V := Y∖G`
non-empty) was dropped when `_hf` was dropped.

## The fix (signature refactor — thread `_hf`; do NOT fill proofs)

The collapse data is, verbatim from `rigidity_lemma`'s current signature (L233–235):

```
    (y₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Y)
    (z₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Z)
    (_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀)
```

Make exactly these three edits:

1. **`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (L81).** Add `y₀`, `z₀`, `_hf` (the three above) to its
   hypotheses, AFTER `(f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)` and `(x₀ : … ⟶ X)`. Also bring its instance
   set up to the FULL set that `rigidity_core` carries — add
